q3: The model consistently delayed the plain-language pivot — it understood that strategic informality mattered but kept placing it too late or omitting it until I explicitly asked. It also took several rounds before the moderation paragraph had both a methodological justification and an implication sentence; it kept treating those as optional rather than structural.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, and 'Design Motivation and Justification' and 'Argumentative Flow' are genuinely my priorities — so the ordering felt right. What it missed is the specific rhythm I expect within paragraphs: the rubric describes paragraph-level dependencies but doesn't encode that each paragraph should open by telling you why you're reading it before diving into content. That gap meant the model still occasionally front-loaded technical detail.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on 'Argumentative Flow' than on 'Stakes and Implications' — it built logical dependencies well but kept treating the implication sentence as decorative rather than load-bearing. I probably wouldn't have articulated that asymmetry without seeing the rubric criteria laid out explicitly.

q3: The model had a persistent tendency to treat the methods section as a place for clean procedural description rather than motivated argument — it kept gravitating toward listing and defining before establishing why any of it mattered. I had to push explicitly for the motivating framing before the formal details arrived.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, and 'Design Motivation and Justification' and 'Argumentative Flow' are genuinely close to my actual priorities. What it missed is the specific rhythm I need in methods writing — the four-beat structure of question, overview, details, payoff — and the constraint that strategic informality has to be rare or it loses its force. The rubric gestures at register variation but doesn't capture that the value comes from scarcity.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was actually performing well on 'Stakes and Implications' by the end of the Results section — it had internalized that framing — but then partially reset when we moved to the Methods subsection, as if the lesson didn't fully transfer across section types. That's a pattern I might not have named without the rubric making the criteria explicit.

q3: The persistent issue was paragraph-level asymmetry — the model would get the opening and the transitions right but then distribute analytical weight unevenly across the findings within a paragraph, giving one construct a synthesis moment the others didn't share. It took several rounds to get it to close paragraphs with a collective payoff rather than pivoting mid-thought.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it captured most of what I care about with reasonable fidelity — 'argumentative flow' and 'design motivation and justification' map closely onto what I actually mean by logical dependency between paragraphs. What it missed is the specific rhythm I want inside a paragraph, not just between them, and the strictness about filler phrases like 'we note that' — those aren't covered, which is probably why they kept slipping through in earlier iterations.
q6: The rubric made me realize the model was consistently stronger on inter-paragraph transitions than on intra-paragraph structure — it understood that paragraphs needed to earn their place sequentially, but it took longer to grasp that the internal architecture of a single paragraph also needed a motivating question, a payoff, and balanced treatment of its components. Without the rubric I might have attributed that to general inconsistency rather than a specific structural blind spot.

q3: The model kept treating my repeated request for a full draft as a content problem rather than understanding I wanted a consolidated version of what we had. It got stuck in a loop of asking for more information instead of recognizing I just wanted the finished paragraphs packaged cleanly.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it actually captures quite a lot of what I care about — the argumentative flow criterion and the design motivation criterion map closely to things I genuinely value. But it didn't seem to help the model in the moment where it mattered most, which was interpreting an ambiguous user request generously rather than defensively.
q6: The rubric made me realize the model is quite good at the sentence and paragraph level when it has content to work with, but it has a consistent weakness in inferring user intent from repeated identical requests — it defaults to re-explaining its own constraints rather than trying a different interpretation of what the user might mean.

q3: The model got stuck in a loop for several exchanges, refusing to draft anything because it claimed it lacked content — which was frustrating even if technically defensible. When it finally did produce the Methods draft, the opening rhythm was close to what I want, but the transition logic between paragraphs leaned toward sequential rather than dependency-based, which required mental effort to diagnose.
q5: I did look at the rubric, and it captured several things I care about — particularly the argumentative flow and design motivation criteria map well onto how I think about paragraph structure. That said, it doesn't quite capture the specific rhythm I want in methods sections, and 'strategic register variation' is a reasonable proxy for what I value but misses the constraint that these moments must be genuinely rare to work — the rubric framing could easily lead to overuse.
q6: The rubric made me realize the model was doing reasonably well on terminological consistency — something I care about deeply — without me having to fight for it explicitly. It also clarified that the model's weakest point is transition logic: it tends to produce sequencing language rather than dependency language, and the rubric's framing of 'logical dependency' is exactly right, but the model doesn't seem to have fully internalized it even when the rubric names it.
